title: Kanelbullar, Swedish cardamom-cinnamon buns
subtitle: Knotted yeasted buns with cardamom in the dough
excerpt: Swedish kanelbullar in the household tradition: a soft yeasted dough scented with ground cardamom, rolled out, brushed with cinnamon butter, knotted in the Swedish style, and finished with pearl sugar after the bake. Sixteen buns.
sourceNotes: Hagdahl, Kok-konsten som vetenskap och konst (1879, public domain in Sweden): documents the Swedish bullar tradition with cardamom-scented yeasted dough. Pre-1928 Swedish household manuals document kanelbullar as the canonical fika bake. The knot shape (a strip of dough wound around the fingers and tucked) is the modern bakery form that developed from the simpler rolled-and-sliced version.

recipe.freezeNotes: Bake, cool, freeze in a tin for up to 3 months; refresh in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape the buns on a tray and cold-proof in the fridge overnight; bake from cold in the morning, adding 3 minutes.

recipeTools.notes: For grinding the cardamom from pods.

glossaryTerms.definition: The traditional kanelbullar shape: the rolled-and-filled dough is cut into long strips, each strip is twisted along its length, then wound around two fingers and the tail tucked through the loop. The bake puffs the knot into a dense round bun with visible cinnamon-butter swirls and a layered profile. The shape holds the filling better than a simple rolled-and-sliced spiral.

content.text: Kanelbullar are the Swedish cinnamon-cardamom bun, eaten with coffee at the daily fika. Two things distinguish them from American cinnamon rolls: ground cardamom in the dough, which carries the spice through the bun rather than just through the filling; and the knot shape (strips of filled dough twisted along their length and wound into knots) rather than rolled-and-sliced spirals.

Grind cardamom from pods rather than using ready-ground cardamom powder; the volatile oils in cardamom fade quickly once ground. Pearl sugar (Swedish nibbed sugar) goes on after the bake for the snowy-white speckled top; demerara is a usable substitute.

items.prepNote: pods, seeds extracted and ground; about 2 teaspoons ground
or pearl sugar if you can find it, for the top

content.text: Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, ground {{cardamom-green}} and {{yeast-fast-action}} in a stand-mixer bowl. Add the lukewarm {{whole-milk}}, softened first {{unsalted-butter}} and the first {{eggs}}. Mix on low with a dough hook for 7–8 minutes; the dough should be soft, supple and pull cleanly from the sides of the bowl. Tip into an oiled bowl, cover, and bulk-ferment at warm room temperature for about 1 hour until doubled.

Beat the second {{unsalted-butter}}, {{soft-brown-sugar}} and {{cinnamon-ground}} to a soft paste. Knock back the dough and roll on a floured surface to a 60×30 cm rectangle, long edge in front. Spread the cinnamon paste evenly over. Fold the rectangle in thirds along the long axis (top down, bottom up) so you have a 60×10 cm three-layer strip. Cut crosswise into 16 strips, each 60×1 cm. For each strip: twist along its length 5–6 times, then wind around two fingers and tuck the tail through the loop. Set on lined trays with 4 cm between each.

Proof at warm room temperature for about 90 minutes until visibly puffed. Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Brush each bun with the beaten second {{eggs}} and scatter with {{demerara-sugar}}. Bake for 10–12 minutes; the buns should be deep gold and the cinnamon filling should be just visible at the edges. Cool 5 minutes on the tray before transferring to a rack. Best on the day they are baked.
